title: The clock that has been wrong for years
summary: Reflect on a public clock that everyone knows is wrong, and why no one fixes it.
topic: A public clock that is always wrong
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences about a public clock, sign, or object that is wrong but nobody fixes. Describe it, explain how people deal with it, and reflect on why it stays that way. Use present simple, present perfect, and the connectors 'but' and 'although'.
hintStarters: The clock above the market gate has shown the wrong time for as long as…
Everyone in the neighbourhood knows it is…
Although it would be easy to fix, nobody…
Strangely, people still look up at it because…
sampleText: The clock on the tower above our market in Lviv has been three minutes slow for as long as I have lived here. Everyone knows it. My mother knew it when she was young, and the old shopkeepers say it has been wrong since before they were born. The hands move, so the clock is not exactly broken; it just runs a little behind real time. You would think someone would fix it, but nobody does. The strange thing is that people still look up at it all day. They check the wrong clock and then they add three minutes in their heads, almost without thinking. Although the time is wrong, everyone gets the right answer, because they all know the secret. Once, a new repairman climbed up and fixed it correctly, and for one week the clock showed the true time. People got confused. They added three minutes as usual and arrived late for things. So the city quietly let it slip back to being slow, and everyone was happy again. I have started to think the clock is not really for telling the time. It is a landmark, a meeting point, a thing we all share. Being right was never its job.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: for as long as I have lived here
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: kể từ khi tôi sống ở đây

sampleAnnotations.phrase: it has been wrong since before they were born
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nó đã sai từ trước khi họ ra đời

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the clock is not exactly broken
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đồng hồ không hẳn là hỏng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: it just runs a little behind real time
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nó chỉ chạy chậm hơn thời gian thật một chút

sampleAnnotations.phrase: people still look up at it all day
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: người ta vẫn ngước nhìn nó cả ngày

sampleAnnotations.phrase: add three minutes in their heads
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: cộng thêm ba phút trong đầu
họ đều biết bí mật ấy

sampleAnnotations.phrase: let it slip back to being slow
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: để nó trở lại chạy chậm

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Being right was never its job
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đúng giờ chưa bao giờ là việc của nó

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'Although' for contrast inside one sentence
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Although the time is wrong, everyone gets the right answer' dùng 'although' để mở đầu một mệnh đề tương phản. Khác với 'but' (nối hai mệnh đề ngang nhau), 'although' đứng đầu một mệnh đề phụ và cho ý ngược với mệnh đề chính.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'Although the time is wrong, everyone gets the right answer' uses 'although' to begin a clause that contrasts. Unlike 'but' (which joins two equal clauses), 'although' starts a subordinate clause and gives an idea opposite to the main clause.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'for' and 'since'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'The clock has been three minutes slow for as long as I have lived here' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành với 'for' để chỉ một khoảng thời gian kéo dài đến nay. Còn 'it has been wrong since before they were born' dùng 'since' để chỉ điểm bắt đầu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'The clock has been three minutes slow for as long as I have lived here' uses the present perfect with 'for' to show a period lasting until now. Meanwhile, 'it has been wrong since before they were born' uses 'since' to mark a starting point.

sampleTranslationVi: Chiếc đồng hồ trên tháp phía trên khu chợ của chúng tôi ở Lviv đã chậm ba phút kể từ khi tôi sống ở đây. Ai cũng biết. Mẹ tôi biết điều đó khi bà còn trẻ, và những người bán hàng lớn tuổi nói nó đã sai từ trước khi họ ra đời. Các kim vẫn chạy, nên đồng hồ không hẳn là hỏng; nó chỉ chạy chậm hơn thời gian thật một chút. Bạn sẽ nghĩ ai đó sẽ sửa nó, nhưng không ai sửa. Điều kỳ lạ là người ta vẫn ngước nhìn nó cả ngày. Họ xem chiếc đồng hồ sai rồi cộng thêm ba phút trong đầu, gần như không cần nghĩ. Mặc dù thời gian sai, ai cũng có đáp án đúng, vì họ đều biết bí mật ấy. Một lần, một thợ sửa mới trèo lên và chỉnh đúng nó, và trong một tuần đồng hồ hiển thị thời gian đúng. Người ta bối rối. Họ vẫn cộng thêm ba phút như thường lệ và đến trễ trong các việc. Vậy là thành phố lặng lẽ để nó trở lại chạy chậm, và ai cũng vui trở lại. Tôi bắt đầu nghĩ rằng đồng hồ không thật sự để xem giờ. Nó là một điểm mốc, một điểm hẹn, một thứ mà tất cả chúng tôi cùng chia sẻ. Đúng giờ chưa bao giờ là việc của nó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best-written sentence about how long the clock has been slow.
options: The clock has been three minutes slow since years.
The clock has been three minutes slow for years.
The clock is three minutes slow for years.
The clock has being three minutes slow for years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'has been' with 'for years' is correct. 'Since years' is wrong (use 'for'); present simple does not show the continuing period; 'has being' uses the wrong form.
mcQuestions.hint: Do we use 'for' or 'since' with a period like 'years'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the most natural sentence using 'although' for contrast.
options: Although the time is wrong, but everyone gets the right answer.
Although the time is wrong, so everyone gets the right answer.
Although the time is wrong, everyone gets the right answer.
Although the time is wrong, everyone get the right answer.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although' alone introduces the contrast. Adding 'but' or 'so' is redundant/incorrect; 'everyone get' drops the third-person 's'.
mcQuestions.hint: Can you use both 'although' and 'but' in the same sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence about the clock not being completely broken.
options: The clock is not exactly broken; it just runs a little behind then.
The clock is not exact broken; it just runs a little behind.
The clock is not exactly broken; it just run a little behind.
The clock is not exactly broken; it just runs a little behind.
mcQuestions.explanation: Adverb 'exactly' and 'runs' are correct. Option 2 adds an odd 'then'; 'exact' is an adjective, not an adverb; 'run' drops the third-person 's'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the best sentence about people checking the clock.
options: People still look up at it all day.
People still look up it all day.
People still look up at it all the day.
People still looks up at it all day.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Look up at it' and 'all day' are correct. Option 2 drops 'at'; 'all the day' adds a wrong article; 'looks' disagrees with 'people'.
mcQuestions.hint: Do we say 'all day' or 'all the day'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about adding minutes mentally.
options: They add three minutes in their heads, almost without to think.
They add three minutes in their heads, almost without thinking.
They add three minutes on their heads, almost without thinking.
They add three minutes in their head, almost without think.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Without thinking' (preposition + -ing) is correct, as is 'in their heads'. Option 2 uses 'to think'; option 3 uses 'on'; option 4 uses bare 'think'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'without', do we use 'thinking' or 'to think'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best sentence about the repairman fixing it.
options: A new repairman climbed up and fixed it correct.
A new repairman climbed up and fix it correctly.
A new repairman climbed up and fixed it correctly.
A new repairman climbed up and fixed it correctness.
mcQuestions.explanation: Adverb 'correctly' modifies 'fixed'. 'Correct' is an adjective; 'fix' should be past 'fixed'; 'correctness' is a noun, not an adverb.
mcQuestions.hint: To describe how he fixed it, use an adjective or an adverb?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about people being confused.
options: People got confusing and arrived late for things.
People got confused and arrived lately for things.
People got confused and arrived late at things.
People got confused and arrived late for things.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Got confused' (people feel it) and 'arrived late' are correct. 'Confusing' describes the cause, not the people; 'lately' means recently; 'late at things' uses the wrong preposition.

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the best sentence about the city letting the clock stay slow.
options: The city quietly let it slip back to being slow.
The city quiet let it slip back to being slow.
The city quietly let it to slip back to being slow.
The city quietly letted it slip back to being slow.
mcQuestions.explanation: Adverb 'quietly' and 'let it slip' (no 'to') are correct. 'Quiet' is an adjective; 'let it to slip' adds a wrong 'to'; 'letted' is wrong ('let' is irregular).
mcQuestions.hint: After 'let' + object, do we add 'to' before the next verb?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about the clock as a meeting point.
options: It is a landmark, a meeting point, a thing we all shares.
It is a landmark, a meeting point, a thing we all share.
It is a landmark, a meeting point, a thing which we all share it.
It is a landmark, a meeting point, a thing that we all sharing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A thing we all share' is correct. 'We all shares' disagrees; 'which we all share it' adds an extra object; 'sharing' is not a full verb here.
mcQuestions.hint: With 'we' as the subject, what form does 'share' take?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best closing sentence about the clock's real purpose.
options: To be right was never it's job.
Being right was never his job.
Being right was never its job.
Be right was never its job.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Being right' as a subject and 'its' (possessive) are correct. 'It's' means 'it is'; 'his' is wrong for a clock; 'Be right' cannot start the subject this way.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why do communities sometimes keep something broken or imperfect rather than fixing it? Can a wrong thing become useful simply because everyone agrees on how it is wrong?
